text: The surgeon performs a limited mobilization of the sigmoid colon due to adherence to inguinal hernia mesh, preventing the lifting of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. Visible is a wad of epiploic fat within a peritoneal hernia defect, which the surgeon is working to reduce.

The surgeon works underneath the IMA pedicle, sweeping nerves posteriorly and isolating the pedicle proximal to the left colic. Fat is meticulously cleaned off the vessel before dividing the IMA, ensuring a good seal. The ureter is visible posteriorly, protected by a sponge, as the surgeon performs a TME dissection in the upper rectum for the distal margin. The lateral dissection begins, joining the medially dissected plane, with the sponge marking the ureter for protection.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization using a vessel sealer, working along Tolt's fascia. The surgeon spreads and cauterizes tissue, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The spleen is briefly visible during the procedure.

The surgeon assesses the tension and length of the colon, deciding to take the mesentery to the proximal margin without mobilizing the IMV for additional length. The specimen contains thirty-six lymph nodes, two of which are positive. The surgeon proceeds to take the distal margin, aiming to remove a separate polyp located distal to the tumor, marked by a tattoo approximately six centimeters away.

The surgeon prepares for an intracorporeal anastomosis by distally and proximally stapling the colon, isolating the segment containing the tumor. The bulky tumor and mesentery prevent transrectal extraction, leading to a decision to perform a sewn two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon staples the proximal and distal ends of the colon segment, preparing for an anastomosis. A proctotomy and enterotomy are made, leaving a margin of tissue between the otomy and staple line. Using ICG, the surgeon assesses perfusion, noting inadequate blood supply on the colon side, prompting a conversion to an end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis, using a vessel sealer to take the mesentery and scissors to divide the bowel wall. The proximal side is already divided, and the distal side is prepared for division with scissors. The specimen is extracted through the rectum, serving as a new proximal and distal margin. The surgeon checks the tension and begins sewing a single-layer end-to-end anastomosis, starting with a 3-0 V-Loc suture to align the midline and invert the mucosa of the posterior lip. ICG is checked to ensure no revisions are needed, and the mucosa is visibly pooched upwards due to the suture placement.

The surgeon begins sewing the posterior aspect of the anastomosis using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, starting on the patient's left side. The surgeon alternates between using the left and right hands to achieve optimal angles for suturing. The fenestrated bipolar instrument is used instead of a second needle driver. The surgeon carefully loops the suture and prepares to start cannulating, turning the needle to bring it to the outside. The anterior lip is left open initially to maintain visibility of the posterior anastomosis. A second 3-0 V-Loc suture is started in the posterior midline, extending around the right side.
